content:
Let the big circle \(C_2\) have center \(O=(0,0)\) and radius \(9\), and let the small circle \(C_1\) have center \(O_1=(9-r,0)\) and radius \(r\).  Then the point of internal tangency is \(A=(9,0)\).  A generic point \(P=(x,y)\) on \(C_1\) satisfies two conditions:

1.  \(P\) lies on \(C_1\):
   \((x-(9-r))^2+y^2=r^2.\)
2.  \(OP\) is tangent to \(C_1\), so
   \((x,y)\cdot\bigl(x-(9-r),y\bigr)=0\)
   \(\;\Longrightarrow\;x^2+y^2+(r-9)x=0\)
   \(\;\Longrightarrow\;x^2+y^2=(9-r)x.\)

From those two equations one finds
  \(x=\dfrac{9(9-2r)}{9-r}\).  Along the ray \(OP\), the big‐circle meets again at
\[
Q\;:\;t=\frac{9}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}},\quad 
\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=\sqrt{(9-r)x}\,.
\]
The tangent at \(A\) is the vertical line \(x=9\), which meets \(OP\) at 
\[
R\;:\;t_R=\frac{9}{x}.
\]
The condition \(PQ=QR\) becomes
\[
(T-1)\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=(t_R-T)\sqrt{x^2+y^2},\quad T=\frac{9}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}}.
\]
That simplifies (after some algebra) to
\[
r(4-r)=0\;\Longrightarrow\;r=4.
\]
Hence the distance from \(O\) to \(P\) is
\[
OP=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}
=\sqrt{(9-r)x}
=\sqrt{5\cdot\frac{9}{5}}=3.
\]
Answer: \(\displaystyle\boxed{3}.\)

message.content:
